region_of_origin: India (Indo-Chinese fusion, popular in Indian restaurants)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indian-style non-vegetarian hot and sour soup. FOODON_03311069 (soup food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Soups and broths) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for Indian-style non-vegetarian hot and sour soup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789007 (Soup) used as closest parent; no specific term for Indian non-vegetarian hot and sour soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of hot and sour soup is approximately 300 g (one bowl), as typically served in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and sour soup, with meat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian food composition data for Indo-Chinese soup preparations
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indian-restaurant-style non-vegetarian hot and sour soup exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Soup, hot and sour, Chinese restaurant' (FDC ID 173581) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN data for chicken-based soups. The Indian restaurant version typically has similar macronutrient profile to Chinese restaurant hot and sour soup with meat, with moderate protein from chicken/egg, low fat, moderate carbohydrate from cornstarch thickening, and elevated sodium from soy sauce.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed soups and broth-based dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for thickened soups and cornstarch-based preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian-style non-vegetarian hot and sour soup. GI estimated at 35 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for thickened broth-based soups (GI range 25–45) and cornstarch-thickened preparations. The low GI reflects the predominantly liquid matrix, moderate cornstarch content, vinegar (which lowers GI), and protein/fat from meat and egg. GL per serving calculated as (4.2 g carbohydrate per 100g × 300g serving / 100) × GI/100 = 12.6g carbohydrate × 0.35 = 4.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index due to liquid matrix, vinegar, and moderate cornstarch thickening
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot and sour soup has a low estimated GI of 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 4.4 per serving, making it a suitable soup choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without additional high-GI accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from egg and meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content (approximately 1260 mg per serving) and moderate cholesterol (approximately 84 mg per serving), and should prefer smaller portions or home-prepared versions with reduced soy sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Non-Vegetarian Hot and Sour Soup is a popular Indo-Chinese soup served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and India. It is a thick, tangy, and spicy broth made by simmering chicken pieces or mixed meat with mushrooms, tofu, egg ribbons, and vegetables in a stock seasoned with soy sauce, white pepper, chilli, ginger, and garlic, then thickened with cornstarch and acidified with vinegar to achieve its characteristic hot and sour flavour profile. The soup has a glossy, viscous texture with a sharp vinegary tang balanced by the heat of white pepper and chilli. It is typically served as a starter or light meal. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (approximately 38 kcal per 100 g) and fat (1.2 g per 100 g), with moderate protein (2.8 g per 100 g) from chicken and egg, and moderate carbohydrate (4.2 g per 100 g) from cornstarch thickening. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content from soy sauce, which can be problematic for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) makes it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is dairy-free and typically halal when prepared with halal-certified meat. The dish is a staple starter at Indian restaurants in Singapore, including establishments such as Zaffron Tandoori.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indian-restaurant-style non-vegetarian hot and sour soup exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from the closest USDA FoodData Central entry for Chinese restaurant hot and sour soup with meat (FDC ID 173581) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN chicken soup data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database thickened soup entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food type (thickened broth-based meat soup) is well-characterised nutritionally and the estimation basis is defensible.
notes: Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central 'Soup, hot and sour, Chinese restaurant' (FDC ID 173581) and ICMR-NIN chicken soup data; no exact Indian-restaurant-style non-vegetarian hot and sour soup entry exists in approved primary sources. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source for this soup type. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source for this soup type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source for this soup type. Trans fat is 0.0 as this is a broth-based soup with no added hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per USDA reference. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous thickened soup entries; vinegar content further supports low GI classification. GL per 300 g serving = 12.6 g carbohydrate × 0.35 = 4.4. Sodium is notably high due to soy sauce; a 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium. The dish is served at Zaffron Tandoori - Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey source). Halal status assumed based on Indian Muslim restaurant context but should be verified per establishment. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'Non-Vegetarian (Hot & Sour Soup)' to the canonical form 'Hot and Sour Soup (Non-Vegetarian)'.
